title: St. Processus & St. Martinian, Martyrs of Rome
narrative:
On the second day of Hamle, the Ethiopian Church commemorates Saints Processus and Martinian, two Roman soldiers who according to tradition were assigned to guard the Apostle Peter during his imprisonment in the Mamertine Prison and were converted by him. The account preserved in the Roman and Ethiopian martyrologies tells how Peter, praying in the dungeon, caused a spring of water to flow from the rock, and with this water baptised his guards along with forty-seven other prisoners. When the conversion became known to the authorities, Processus and Martinian were arrested, tortured, and beheaded — dying for the same faith they had received only days or weeks before.

The witness of Processus and Martinian belongs to a pattern the Ethiopian Synaxarium finds deeply significant: conversion and martyrdom in swift succession, with no long period of catechumenal formation between the reception of faith and its ultimate testing. This pattern recurs in the accounts of the soldier-converts at the martyrdoms of many other saints, and the Ethiopian tradition interprets it as evidence of the particular grace that accompanies proximity to a holy person. To be near a true confessor of Christ — even in the role of jailer, even in the context of coercion — is to be exposed to a light that can transform a soul in an instant.

There is a further dimension to this feast that the Ethiopian Church has always honoured: the witness of guards and jailers and soldiers who chose conscience above command. In a world where military and imperial service demanded religious conformity as an expression of political loyalty, the decision of Processus and Martinian to accept baptism was simultaneously a religious conversion and an act of insubordination. They chose to be subject to the One whom their prisoner confessed above the authority of the emperor who employed them. The Ethiopian Church, which has known many periods in which the faithful were required to choose between earthly and heavenly allegiance, venerates these Roman soldiers as brothers in this particular form of courage. Amen.
